saying: Red sky at night, sailor's delight; red sky at morning, sailor take warning.
attribution: John Augustine and Lisa Smith, NOAA Global Monitoring Laboratory, "Red Sky in the Morning" (gml.noaa.gov/grad/about/redsky/); corroborated by Scientific American, "Is there scientific validity to the saying...?" and the Hong Kong Observatory weather-basics explainer.
explanation: Real atmospheric science grounds this saying, and it holds with a stated caveat. NOAA explains the mechanism: in the mid-latitude belt of the westerlies, weather systems move west-to-east; high pressure produces descending, warming air that traps aerosols, creating a "dirty" atmosphere that scatters short (blue) wavelengths away and lets long (red) wavelengths through when the low sun's light travels a long atmospheric path. A RED SUNSET (looking west, toward incoming weather) means high pressure / clearing air is approaching from the west -> fair weather ahead = sailor's delight. A RED SUNRISE (looking east, toward departing weather) means the high pressure has already passed east and a low-pressure storm system is likely moving in from the west -> warning. The load-bearing caveat, stated explicitly by NOAA: this only works in the westerlies, roughly 30-60 degrees latitude in both hemispheres; within ~30 degrees of the equator easterly trade winds dominate and the prediction can invert. So: genuinely true within its domain, not universal.

saying: Lightning never strikes the same place twice.
attribution: U.S. National Weather Service (NOAA), "Lightning Myths" (weather.gov/safety/lightning-myths) and NOAA "5 striking facts versus myths about lightning you should know"; Encyclopaedia Britannica, "Can Lightning Strike the Same Place Twice?"; Guinness World Records (Roy Sullivan, seven strikes 1942-1977).
explanation: This is a documented myth, explicitly refuted by named meteorological authorities. The U.S. National Weather Service lists it on its official "Lightning Myths" page (weather.gov/safety/lightning-myths) and NOAA's "5 striking facts versus myths about lightning" both state plainly that lightning often strikes the same place repeatedly, especially tall, pointed, isolated objects. The mechanism: lightning seeks the shortest, most conductive path to ground; when a downward stepped leader approaches, tall grounded objects emit upward streamers that preferentially connect the channel, so the same favorable point is struck again and again. Concrete documented counterexamples: the Empire State Building is hit roughly 23-25 times per year (NWS) and was struck eight times in 24 minutes during one storm; One World Trade Center was struck 189 times between 2015 and 2020. For people: park ranger Roy Sullivan was struck seven times (1942-1977), recognized by Guinness World Records. The saying is false; it survives only as a figure of speech about improbable coincidence, not as physics.

saying: Plant corn when the oak leaves are the size of a squirrel's ear.
attribution: Roger Elmore, "Influence of Soil Temperature on Corn Germination and Growth," Iowa State University Extension Integrated Crop Management (2012) — corn germination minimum of 50 F soil; and Joe Boggs / Dan Herms (OSU Phenology Calendar), Ohio State University BYGL, "Observations on Phenology" — oak-leaf indicator timing via Growing Degree Days. Mechanism corroborated by Pioneer/Purdue (50 F threshold) and the growing-degree-day phenology literature.
explanation: This is a sound phenological proxy, grounded by named extension science. Oak leaf-out and corn-safe soil are both driven by the SAME variable: accumulated spring warmth, measured as growing degree days (GDD). Corn's hard requirement is a seed-zone soil temperature of about 50 F; below it, seeds imbibe water but the radicle and coleoptile do not grow, leaving them to rot or be attacked by pathogens (Roger Elmore, Professor of Agronomy, Iowa State University Extension, 2012: "With soil temperatures below 50 F, seeds readily absorb water but do not initiate root or shoot growth"). The same accumulated heat that pushes soil up through that 50 F range also drives oak buds to leaf out, so a small ("squirrel's/mouse's ear") oak leaf is a free, local, self-calibrating signal that the soil has warmed and late-frost risk has receded — more reliable than a fixed calendar date because, as OSU's BYGL (Joe Boggs, citing the OSU Phenology Calendar by Dan Herms) notes, "the seasonal appearance of important events can vary from year-to-year" while phenological events track accumulated heat. The stated CAVEAT: it is a coarse directional cue, not a precise instrument — oak species, latitude, and microclimate shift the timing, and folk versions disagree ("squirrel's ear" vs. the equally common "mouse's ear"). It points the right way; it does not replace a soil thermometer. Indigenous farmers originated the observation and taught it to settlers. So: true, with a caveat — it works because oak phenology and corn-germination soil temperature are both readouts of the same accumulated heat.

saying: Knee-high by the Fourth of July.
attribution: Joe Lauer, Agronomy Professor, University of Wisconsin-Madison (quoted in Wisconsin State Farmer / wisfarmer.com, 2025, and Madison.com "Curiosities"); Iowa Corn Growers Association (8 ft by midsummer); corroborated by Brownfield Ag News, "Modern genetics, fertility make the old 'knee high' saying obsolete."
explanation: As a benchmark for a healthy, on-track corn crop, this is documented as obsolete by named agricultural scientists. The adage dates to roughly the 19th/early-20th century, when corn was planted mid-to-late May and knee-high (~18-24 in.) by July 4 signaled a normal crop. That is no longer true. Joe Lauer, agronomy professor at the University of Wisconsin-Madison, states flatly: "Nowadays, if corn is knee-high by the Fourth of July, it's way behind," and that modern corn should be chest- or neck-high by then. The Iowa Corn Growers Association notes that under good conditions Iowa corn reaches about 8 feet tall by midsummer. The mechanism of the change is concrete and verifiable: improved hybrid vigor and genetics, better soil fertility, seed treatments, and modern pest management, plus much earlier planting (late April/early May today vs. mid-to-late May in the 1950s-60s), all of which push July-4 height far past the knee. So as a literal performance standard the saying is FALSE for modern row-crop corn -- knee-high corn on July 4 now signals a lagging crop, not a healthy one. (Caveat: it survives only as nostalgic folklore and as a rough rhyme, not as an agronomic gauge.)

saying: An apple a day keeps the doctor away.
attribution: Davis MA, Bynum JPW, Sirovich BE. "Association Between Apple Consumption and Physician Visits: Appealing the Conventional Wisdom That an Apple a Day Keeps the Doctor Away." JAMA Internal Medicine, 2015 (PMID 25822137).
explanation: The literal claim was directly tested and refuted. Davis, Bynum & Sirovich (JAMA Internal Medicine, 2015) used NHANES 2007-2010 data (n=8,728) to compare daily apple eaters (at least 149 g/day, ~1 small apple) with non-apple-eaters. The crude data showed apple eaters slightly more likely to avoid the doctor (39.0% vs 33.9%), but after adjusting for sociodemographic and health-related characteristics (apple eaters were better educated, less likely to smoke, etc.), the difference in physician visits was NOT statistically significant. So the proverb's literal core — that daily apple consumption reduces doctor visits — does not hold: the apparent benefit was confounding, not the apple. The authors found only one modest surviving association — daily apple eaters used slightly fewer prescription medications ("kept the pharmacist away," also non-significant on full adjustment) — which does not rescue the "doctor away" claim. Note the study was deliberately published in JAMA Internal Medicine's tongue-in-cheek issue, but its methods and null finding are real and peer-reviewed. The proverb is a 19th-century rhyme (traced to Pembrokeshire, Wales, 1860s), not a grounded health claim; apples are nutritious, but they do not measurably keep the doctor away.

saying: It takes a thousand bees to make a pound of honey.
explanation: The saying is a memorable round-number approximation of a genuinely computable quantity, and the published figures land in the same order of magnitude but below a thousand. The standard derivation uses two constants: a pound of honey is about 64 teaspoons, and a worker bee makes roughly 1/12 teaspoon of honey in her lifetime (the figure cited by the Indiana Department of Natural Resources and Penn State Horticulture). That yields 64 x 12 = about 768 bees per pound, consistent with the widely repeated 556-768 range. So "a thousand bees" overstates the most-cited estimate by roughly 30%, but is the right magnitude as folk shorthand. Crucially there is NO settled single literature verdict, because the per-bee lifetime input is itself soft and disputed: the Canadian Honey Council states a bee makes "about 1 and 1/2 teaspoons" in its lifetime (implying only ~43 bees per pound), an order of magnitude from the 1/12-teaspoon sources. Multiple sources note the per-bee amount is an average that cannot actually be measured for any individual bee. Because the verdict turns on a division of two looked-up constants rather than settled empirical research, this is best left as a deterministic computation (VERIFIED) rather than ATTRIBUTED as a fact or RETIRED as a myth: the claim is neither cleanly true at exactly 1000 nor false as a myth, but an approximation whose core is checkable arithmetic. Sources: https://honeycouncil.ca/how-to-make-a-pound-of-honey/ ; https://www.in.gov/dnr/kids/animals/insects/honeybees ; https://pennhort.libguides.com/beekeeping ; https://www.beverlybees.com/honey-bee-facts/
verifier: Deterministic check: bees_per_pound = teaspoons_per_pound / honey_per_bee_lifetime_tsp, then compare to the asserted count within a tolerance band. Inputs are two looked-up constants. Example spec: input {teaspoons_per_pound: 64, honey_per_bee_lifetime_tsp: 0.0833 (=1/12), claimed_bees: 1000, tolerance: 2x}; compute 64 / 0.0833 = 768; verdict APPROXIMATE because 768 is within 2x of claimed 1000 (ratio 1.30). A second row {honey_per_bee_lifetime_tsp: 1.5} yields 43 bees, flagging that the lifetime constant is itself uncertain across sources and the result is sensitive to which value is used. The verifier should report both the computed count and the source-disagreement spread, not a single boolean.

saying: What goes up must come down.
attribution: Newton's law of universal gravitation (Isaac Newton, Principia, 1687); escape velocity for Earth ≈ 11.186 km/s as stated by standard physics references (Wikipedia "Escape velocity"; NASA imagine.gsfc.nasa.gov).
explanation: The saying holds for ordinary objects and is grounded in Newton's law of universal gravitation: any projectile launched upward in Earth's gravitational field at less than escape velocity is decelerated by gravity, stops, and falls back — so for everyday throws it is reliably true. The mechanism is a real, named one and the saying is genuinely explained by it. The required caveat is that it is NOT universally true: an object reaching or exceeding escape velocity (~11.186 km/s at Earth's surface) is slowed by an ever-weakening gravitational pull but only asymptotically approaches zero speed as distance approaches infinity, and therefore never returns. Real spacecraft (e.g., interplanetary probes) launched above this threshold have left Earth permanently. So the saying is true under everyday conditions and false above escape velocity — an ATTRIBUTED holding with a clearly stated caveat, not a universal law.
